items.correct: The stiffness of a material — the ratio of stress to strain in the elastic region.
items.misconception: Young's modulus measures how strong a material is — how much force it can take before breaking.
items.random_wrong: Young's modulus measures the density of a material.
items.why: Students conflate stiffness (resistance to deformation) with strength (resistance to failure) because both feel like 'tough'.

items.q: Is ultraviolet light visible to humans?
items.correct: No — even though it sits next to violet on the spectrum, its frequency is above the range our eyes detect.
items.misconception: Yes — UV is visible because it's right next to violet.
items.random_wrong: Yes — UV is the colour purple.
items.why: Students assume adjacency on the EM spectrum implies visibility; they don't appreciate the sharp biological cut-off at around 400 nm.

items.q: What force keeps a car moving in a circle around a bend?
items.correct: Friction from the road acting towards the centre of the circle — centripetal force.
items.misconception: A centrifugal force pushes the car outwards from the centre.
items.random_wrong: The car's own momentum acts as a force that drives it in a circle.
items.why: Students experience the 'outward push' in a car (a result of inertia in a rotating frame) and interpret it as a real outward force.

items.q: Faraday's law of induction was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1831. “1801” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If transformer has Np = 1000, Ns = 100 and Vp = 240 V AC, Vs equals:
items.why: It's 24 V. “240 V” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Exact answer: 1831 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
